retrieved_docs.title: Unstable Angina -- Complications
retrieved_docs.content: Although unstable angina is associated with lower mortality and relative incidence than NSTEMI, the risk of subsequent nonfatal myocardial infarction remains comparable. [32] Patients with unstable angina demonstrate a lower 1-year risk of death and MACE than those with NSTEMI. However, when compared to patients with stable angina undergoing coronary angiography without a prior history of coronary artery disease, unstable angina is associated with a nonsignificant difference in MACE risk but a higher risk of death. [33]

retrieved_docs.title: Unstable Angina -- History and Physical
retrieved_docs.content: A key distinguishing feature of unstable angina is incomplete resolution of pain with typical relieving factors. Many patients have preexisting CAD, either previously diagnosed or with stable anginal symptoms. These patients often report increased frequency, duration, or severity of chest pain episodes; such changes suggest unstable angina rather than stable or other causes of chest pain. Recognizing these symptoms is critical, as unstable angina may indicate impending myocardial infarction or ST-elevation myocardial infarction, necessitating urgent evaluation due to higher morbidity and mortality risks compared to stable angina. [18]

retrieved_docs.title: Stable Angina -- Prognosis
retrieved_docs.content: The prognosis varies depending on the etiology of stable angina. Regardless of the etiology, aggressive risk factor modification is imperative. In individuals with stable angina, screening for increased frequency of symptoms or transition to unstable angina should be routinely performed.

retrieved_docs.title: Unstable Angina -- Introduction
retrieved_docs.content: Unstable angina falls under the umbrella term "acute coronary syndrome" (ACS). ACS affects a large portion of the population and remains the leading cause of death worldwide. [1] Distinguishing unstable angina from other causes of chest pain, including stable angina, guides treatment decisions and patient disposition. Clinicians must recognize the signs and symptoms of ACS, as patients depend on healthcare professionals to differentiate this condition from noncardiac chest pain. [2] Although patients frequently present to the emergency department, ACS can also manifest in the outpatient setting. Extensive research has identified effective treatment modalities and diagnostic tools essential for managing unstable angina and other ACS variants. [3] [4] [5]

retrieved_docs.title: Unstable Angina -- Etiology
retrieved_docs.content: Coronary atherosclerotic disease underlies unstable angina in nearly all patients with acute myocardial ischemia. The primary cause of unstable angina involves coronary artery narrowing due to a nonocclusive thrombus forming on a disrupted atherosclerotic plaque. [6] A less common cause is coronary artery vasospasm, as seen in variant Prinzmetal angina. Endothelial or vascular smooth muscle dysfunction triggers this vasospasm (see Image. Coronary Artery Changes and Corresponding Types of Angina). [7]

retrieved_docs.title: Angina -- Introduction
retrieved_docs.content: Angina, or chest pain, is the most common symptom of ischemic heart disease, a major cause of morbidity and mortality worldwide. Chest pain can be due to non-cardiac and cardiac causes, and thorough history and physical is critical in differentiating these causes and identifying patients experiencing acute coronary syndrome. Angina is one of the signs of acute coronary syndrome (ACS) and can further subdivide into stable and unstable angina. Stable angina defines as the occurrence of symptoms with exertion only. Unstable angina or symptoms occurring at rest requires more prompt evaluation and management. Approximately 9 million patients in the United States have symptoms of angina, and recognizing these symptoms is imperative in improving patient outcomes. [1]

retrieved_docs.title: Coronary Artery Disease -- History and Physical
retrieved_docs.content: Angina pectoris may be classified into 2 types: stable and unstable. Stable angina is generally triggered by physical activity, whereas unstable angina can occur suddenly, even at rest. Patients with unstable angina have a poorer prognosis and a higher risk of progressing to myocardial infarction. Individuals with CAD may exhibit either typical (classic) or atypical (nonclassic) pain. Classic pain generally presents as midsternal discomfort, sometimes radiating to the left neck, shoulder, or arm. However, in some cases, the only symptom may be craniofacial pain during an ischemic event.

retrieved_docs.title: Unstable Angina -- Prognosis
retrieved_docs.content: The critical complications of unstable angina include the following: Myocardial infarction Heart failure Ventricular arrhythmia Cardiac arrest

retrieved_docs.title: Unstable Angina -- Epidemiology
retrieved_docs.content: Coronary artery disease (CAD) represents the most common form of atherosclerotic cardiovascular disease and caused approximately 371,506 deaths in 2022; adults younger than 65 accounted for 20% of cardiovascular disease deaths in the same year. Among adults aged 20 and older, 1 in 20 has CAD. Every 40 seconds, 1 person in the United States (US) experiences a heart attack. (Source: Centers for Disease Control and Prevention , 2024) CAD causes over a third of the deaths in individuals aged 35 and older, representing the leading cause of mortality in this age group.

retrieved_docs.title: Unstable Angina (Nursing) -- Introduction
retrieved_docs.content: Unstable angina falls along a spectrum under the umbrella term acute coronary syndrome. This public health issue that daily affects a large portion of the population remains the leading cause of death worldwide. Distinguishing between this and other causes of chest pain that include stable angina is important regarding treatment and disposition of the patient. Providers should be aware of the signs and symptoms of acute coronary syndrome as patients rely on health care professionals to make the distinction from other causes of chest pain. Often patients will present to the emergency room. However, acute coronary syndrome can be seen in the outpatient setting as well. Over the years, a significant amount of research has gone into determining the appropriate and most effective treatment modalities, as well as the diagnostic tools available, in evaluating unstable angina and the other variants of acute coronary syndrome. [1] , [2] , [3]

retrieved_docs.title: Stable Angina -- Introduction
retrieved_docs.content: Stable angina, also known as typical angina or angina pectoris, is a symptom of myocardial ischemia. Stable angina is characterized by chest discomfort or anginal equivalent that is provoked with exertion and alleviated at rest or with nitroglycerin. This is often 1 of the first manifestations or warning signs of underlying coronary disease. Angina affects 10 million people in the United States (US); given this, it is important to recognize the signs and symptoms and appropriately risk stratify and manage these individuals. [1]

retrieved_docs.title: Unstable Angina -- History and Physical
retrieved_docs.content: Current guidelines recommend using risk-stratification tools to assess prognosis and guide management. [20] Commonly employed risk scores include the Thrombolysis in Myocardial Infarction risk score for non-ST-elevation myocardial infarction and the Global Registry of Acute Coronary Events risk score. [21] [22]

retrieved_docs.title: Unstable Angina -- Pearls and Other Issues
retrieved_docs.content: Legally, unstable angina and other forms of ACS represent a significant proportion of cases brought against clinicians. This challenge has led to aggressive evaluation of chest pain in general, resulting in high rates of hospital admissions, extensive testing, and a notable number of false positives that often prompt unnecessary procedures. Over the years, several clinical decision rules have been developed to reduce inappropriate admissions and testing. However, the sensitivity and specificity of these guidelines vary widely. Despite ongoing litigation, clinicians tend to err on the side of caution, maintaining a relatively aggressive approach to the management and treatment of chest pain suggestive of ACS.

retrieved_docs.title: Unstable Angina -- Epidemiology
retrieved_docs.content: Despite significant advancements in diagnosing and treating ACS, cardiovascular disease remains the leading cause of death worldwide, with ischemic heart disease accounting for 50% of these fatalities. [8] Atherosclerotic cardiovascular diseases contribute to 12% of the global annual loss in disability-adjusted life years. [9] Notable global variations exist in rates of revascularization and long-term mortality following ACS. [10] [11]

retrieved_docs.title: Unstable Angina -- Prognosis
retrieved_docs.content: The atherogenic index of plasma, calculated as the logarithm (base 10) of the ratio of triglycerides to high-density lipoprotein cholesterol, serves as a novel biomarker for assessing the risk of coronary heart disease and atherosclerosis. [29] In patients with prediabetes, the atherogenic index of plasma demonstrates a significant correlation with the prognosis of unstable angina. [30] Additionally, exposure to specific air pollutants, particularly carbon monoxide and particulate matter with a diameter of 10 µm or less, has been associated with an increased risk of heart failure readmissions among patients with unstable angina. [31]

retrieved_docs.title: Unstable Angina -- Treatment / Management
retrieved_docs.content: Management primarily aims to increase blood flow in the coronary arteries through diverse modalities. Treatment strategies should be tailored to the patient’s risk profile, symptom severity, and overall clinical presentation.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 13 -- Level 4 -- Community 2885
retrieved_docs.content: Unstable angina (ذبحة غير مستقرة) and chest pain (ألم الصدر) are both clinically associated with hypercholesterolemia (فرط كوليسترول الدم). This relationship indicates that elevated blood cholesterol levels can contribute to the development of acute coronary syndromes such as unstable angina and may manifest as chest discomfort. Recognizing hypercholesterolemia as an underlying factor for these cardiac presentations underscores the importance of lipid screening and management in patients who exhibit chest pain or anginal symptoms, thereby aiding in risk stratification and preventive cardiovascular care.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 20 -- Level 4 -- Community 2829
retrieved_docs.content: Hypercholesterolemia (فرط كوليسترول الدم) is clinically associated with unstable angina (ذبحة غير مستقرة), acute pancreatitis (الْتِهابُ البَنكرِياسِ الحادّ), and chest pain (ألم الصَدْر). These connections indicate that elevated cholesterol levels can contribute to cardiovascular manifestations such as unstable angina and chest pain, while also being linked to inflammatory conditions like acute pancreatitis. Recognizing these relationships supports a more comprehensive evaluation and management strategy for patients with high cholesterol.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 29 -- Level 4 -- Community 2841
retrieved_docs.content: Unstable angina (ذبحة غير مستقرة) and acute pancreatitis (الْتِهابُ البَنكرِياسِ الحادّ) are both clinically associated with hypercholesterolemia (فرط كوليسترول الدم). Hypercholesterolemia, defined by elevated blood cholesterol levels, is a recognized risk factor for atherosclerotic disease that can precipitate unstable angina, and it may also contribute to inflammatory conditions such as acute pancreatitis. Recognizing these connections highlights the importance of effective lipid management to reduce the risk of both cardiovascular events and pancreatic inflammation.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 34 -- Level 3 -- Community 1495
retrieved_docs.content: Coronary artery disease (تصلب الشريان التاجي) is clinically associated with several cardiovascular conditions and diagnostic procedures. It is linked to hypercholesterolemia (فرط كوليسترول الدم), which is a major risk factor for the development of atherosclerotic plaque in the coronary arteries. Coronary artery disease also co‑occurs with congestive heart failure (فشل قلبي احتقاني), reflecting the progression of ischemic damage to cardiac function. Cardiac catheterization (قسطرة قلبية) is frequently employed in the assessment and management of coronary artery disease, as well as in the evaluation of related cardiac disorders (اضطراب قلبي), hypercholesterolemia, chest pain (ألم الصَدْر), and neck pain (ألم في العنق). Cardiac disorders (اضطراب قلبي) themselves show clinical associations with coronary artery disease, hypercholesterolemia, and the use of cardiac catheterization, underscoring the interconnected nature of these pathologies. Chest pain (ألم الصَدْر) is commonly observed in patients with hypercholesterolemia and often prompts cardiac catheterization to rule out or treat underlying coronary artery disease. Neck pain (ألم في العنق) may also be a presenting symptom that leads clinicians to perform cardiac catheterization when cardiac involvement is suspected. Overall, hypercholesterolemia, coronary artery disease, congestive heart failure, various pain presentations, and cardiac catheterization form a tightly linked clinical network, highlighting the importance of integrated diagnostic and therapeutic strategies in cardiovascular patient care.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 33 -- Level 3 -- Community 2406
retrieved_docs.content: the anterior descending branch of the left coronary artery; acute ST segment elevation myocardial infarction due to proximal left anterior descending coronary artery occlusion; and acute ST segment elevation myocardial infarction due to right coronary artery occlusion. Together, these entities illustrate that acute infarct encompasses both Q‑wave and non‑Q‑wave patterns and a variety of STEMI presentations determined by the precise location of coronary artery obstruction, which is essential for accurate diagnosis, risk stratification, and targeted therapeutic management.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 5 -- Level 4 -- Community 2830
retrieved_docs.content: Hypercholesterolemia (فرط كوليسترول الدم) is clinically associated with unstable angina (ذبحة غير مستقرة) and with both type 1 diabetes mellitus (مرض السكري نوع 1) and type 2 diabetes mellitus (مرض السكري نوع 2). This interrelationship indicates that elevated blood cholesterol levels contribute to the development of acute coronary syndromes such as unstable angina and are commonly observed in patients with either form of diabetes. Consequently, careful monitoring and management of lipid levels are essential components of care for individuals with diabetes to mitigate cardiovascular risk.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 15 -- Level 1 -- Community 171
retrieved_docs.content: identification of the occluded coronary territory is emphasized to guide reperfusion and optimize therapeutic strategy in acute coronary syndromes, supporting accurate diagnosis, classification, and targeted management of acute ischemic heart disease.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 4 -- Level 3 -- Community 2367
retrieved_docs.content: myocardial infarction due to right coronary artery occlusion, indicating that the broader category includes infarcts defined simply by the occluded major coronary vessel. The classification further extends to Acute myocardial infarction during procedure, Acute non‑ST segment elevation myocardial infarction (disorder), and Acute widespread myocardial infarction, demonstrating that Acute infarct also captures procedural‑related, non‑ST‑segment elevation, and extensive myocardial injury patterns. Recognizing these diverse entities under the shared morphology of Acute infarct aids clinicians in precise diagnosis, risk stratification, and selection of targeted therapeutic strategies for patients presenting with acute coronary syndromes.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 27 -- Level 2 -- Community 1103
retrieved_docs.content: Acute myocardial infarction encompasses a spectrum of disorders that all involve injury to the myocardium. Specific forms include Acute Q‑wave infarction – widespread, Acute ST‑segment elevation myocardial infarction (STEMI), and Acute STEMI due to occlusion of distinct coronary branches such as the left coronary artery, the left anterior descending (LAD) artery, the circumflex branch of the left coronary artery, the diagonal branch of the LAD, the intermediate artery, the marginal branch of the right coronary artery, the posterior descending branch of the right coronary artery, and the septal branch of the LAD. Additional variants are Acute myocardial infarction due to left coronary artery occlusion, Acute myocardial infarction due to circumflex branch occlusion, Acute myocardial infarction due to right coronary artery occlusion, Acute myocardial infarction occurring during a procedure, and Acute widespread myocardial infarction. Despite the differing anatomic sites of arterial blockage and electrocardiographic patterns (Q‑wave versus ST‑segment elevation), each of these entities shares the common finding site of the myocardium, underscoring that the primary pathological substrate in all these conditions is myocardial tissue damage. Recognizing the specific coronary artery involved helps guide reperfusion strategies and risk stratification, while the universal involvement of the myocardium highlights the need for prompt diagnosis and treatment to preserve cardiac function.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 21 -- Level 4 -- Community 2914
retrieved_docs.content: Chest pain (ألم الصَدْر) is clinically associated with cardiac catheterization (قسطرة قلبية). In clinical practice, the presence of chest pain often prompts consideration of cardiac catheterization to evaluate coronary artery disease, while the procedure itself may produce chest discomfort as a post‑procedural symptom. Recognizing this association helps clinicians diagnose underlying cardiac pathology and manage patient symptoms before, during, and after the intervention.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 19 -- Level 3 -- Community 2559
retrieved_docs.content: Acute Q wave infarction – widespread, acute ST‑segment elevation myocardial infarctions caused by occlusion of the distal left anterior descending coronary artery, the circumflex branch of the left coronary artery, the distal portion of the right coronary artery, the intermediate artery, the marginal branch of the right coronary artery, or the posterior descending branch of the right coronary artery, as well as acute ST‑segment elevation myocardial infarction involving the left anterior descending coronary artery and acute myocardial infarction due to left coronary artery occlusion, all represent distinct presentations of an acute myocardial infarction that share a common pathological substrate in the myocardium. Acute non‑Q wave infarction, acute non‑Q wave infarction – widespread, acute non‑ST‑segment elevation myocardial infarction (disorder), acute widespread myocardial infarction, postoperative acute myocardial infarction, and subsequent anterior non‑ST‑segment elevation myocardial infarction are additional variants of acute myocardial infarction, each reflecting different electrocardiographic patterns, timing, or clinical contexts, yet each also localizes its injury to the myocardial tissue. Collectively, these entities illustrate how diverse coronary artery occlusions, ECG morphologies, and clinical settings converge on myocardial damage as the central finding site, underscoring the importance of recognizing the specific type of infarction for appropriate diagnosis, risk stratification, and management in patient care.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 16 -- Level 3 -- Community 2625
retrieved_docs.content: include Acute myocardial infarction due to occlusion of circumflex branch of left coronary artery, Acute myocardial infarction due to right coronary artery occlusion, Acute non‑ST segment elevation myocardial infarction (disorder), and Acute widespread myocardial infarction. Together, these entities illustrate the diverse anatomic and electrocardiographic presentations that can be associated with an acute infarct, underscoring the need for precise identification of the underlying coronary occlusion to guide appropriate clinical management.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 15 -- Level 3 -- Community 2197
retrieved_docs.content: Acute infarct is a cardiac pathology that can manifest with a wide range of morphologic patterns. It is associated with acute Q wave infarction – including the widespread form – and with acute Q wave myocardial infarction. Various forms of acute ST segment elevation myocardial infarction are also linked to acute infarct, such as the general disorder and specific presentations caused by occlusion of distinct coronary segments: the anterior descending branch of the left coronary artery, the distal portion of the right coronary artery, the mid portion of the right coronary artery, the posterior descending branch of the right coronary artery, and right coronary artery occlusion overall. In addition, acute infarct encompasses acute myocardial infarction due to occlusion of the circumflex branch of the left coronary artery, acute myocardial infarction due to right coronary artery occlusion, and acute myocardial infarction that occurs during a medical procedure. Non‑Q wave morphologies are included as well, with both acute non‑Q wave infarction and its widespread variant being associated with acute infarct. Furthermore, acute infarct is linked to acute non‑ST segment elevation myocardial infarction and to subsequent anterior non‑ST segment elevation myocardial infarction, reflecting its relevance to both initial and later ischemic events. Together, these associations illustrate that acute infarct can present with diverse electrocardiographic and anatomic features, emphasizing the importance of recognizing its various morphologies for accurate diagnosis and management.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 1 -- Level 4 -- Community 2830
retrieved_docs.content: Chest pain (ألم الصَدْر) is clinically associated with hypercholesterolemia (فرط كوليسترول الدم). Elevated blood cholesterol promotes atherosclerotic plaque development in coronary arteries, which can impair myocardial blood flow and present as chest pain. Recognizing this link enables clinicians to assess patients with chest pain for underlying lipid abnormalities and to consider lipid‑lowering interventions as part of comprehensive cardiovascular risk management.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 17 -- Level 3 -- Community 2364
retrieved_docs.content: coronary artery, and Acute ST segment elevation myocardial infarction due to distal portion of the right coronary artery all share the acute infarct morphology. In addition, acute infarct morphology is also associated with Acute myocardial infarction due to left coronary artery occlusion, Acute myocardial infarction due to right coronary artery occlusion, Acute non‑ST segment elevation myocardial infarction (NSTEMI), and Subsequent anterior non‑ST segment elevation myocardial infarction. Together, these relationships illustrate that acute infarct serves as the common pathological substrate linking diverse coronary occlusion sites and both ST‑segment elevation and non‑ST‑segment elevation myocardial infarction presentations, which is essential for accurate diagnosis, risk stratification, and targeted therapeutic management.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 17 -- Level 3 -- Community 1855
retrieved_docs.content: Cocaine use (تعاطي الكوكائين) is clinically associated with unstable angina (ذبحة غير مستقرة), abdominal pain (ألم بطني), and increased blood pressure (زيادة ضغط الدم). The vasoconstrictive and pro‑thrombotic effects of cocaine can precipitate acute myocardial ischemia, manifesting as unstable angina, while its sympathomimetic activity commonly raises systemic arterial pressure. Additionally, cocaine‑induced visceral vasospasm or ischemia may present as abdominal pain. Recognizing these interrelated clinical manifestations is essential for timely diagnosis and appropriate management of patients who use cocaine.
